summary: Cantrill announces the launch of SmartDataCenter, explaining Joyent's transformation from a cloud service operator to a software company that both builds and runs its own product. He reflects on the fundamental tension between building products versus operating services, noting that services can compensate for shortcomings with 'operational heroics' while products must be architecturally sound. Despite engineering conservatism warning against shipping a product and deploying it to production simultaneously, the team's ambitious approach forced company-wide focus and resulted in software that earned genuine praise from battle-hardened operations engineers.
key_points: Joyent transformed from a cloud operator to a software company that both makes and operates cloud software
The transition from service to product is arduous because services can rely on operational heroics that products cannot
Products that survive despite labor-intensive shortcomings do so through professional services - 'caulking' greased by enterprise sales reps
Building software with deep understanding of production operations is essential for creating deployable products
Simultaneous product shipping and production deployment, while risky, created singular company-wide focus
money_quotes: This transition – from service to product – is an arduous one, for the simple reason that a service can make up for any shortcomings with operational heroics that are denied to a product.
That one is unable to throw meat at a system changes many fundamental engineering decisions.

Some products don't internalize these differences and manage to survive despite their labor-intensive shortcomings, but this is thanks to the caulking called 'professional services' – and is usually greased by an enterprise software sales rep with an easy rapport and a hell of a golf handicap.
If a product has no understanding or empathy for how it will be deployed into production, it will be at best difficult to operate, and at worst unacceptable for operation.
We had endeavored to build the product that our team would want to deploy and operate, and it was singularly inspiring to see them so enthusiastic about it!
key_insight: The best infrastructure products emerge when the same team both builds the software and operates it in production, creating a feedback loop that forces architectural decisions a pure service or pure product company would never make.
